coredata.dc:description: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) play dual roles in physiological processes and disease pathogenesis, making their precise regulation crucial for therapeutic applications. Herein, we report the rational design of red-emissive carbon dots (CDs) with light-switchable pro-oxidant/antioxidant bifunctionality for precision nanomedicine. Synthesized from emodin and urea via a solvothermal method, these N-doped CDs exhibit: (1) red emission (>650 nm) with enhanced tissue penetration, (2) efficient type I/II ROS generation under light irradiation, (3) effective ROS scavenging in darkness , and superior water solubility and biocompatibility for in vivo applications. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations confirm that N-doping optimizes the spectral properties of Emo-CDs and promotes the generation of ROS. The unique graphene-like core with abundant surface functional groups enables reversible switching between oxidative and antioxidative states under dark conditions. This work establishes a new paradigm for developing intelligent theranostic agents capable of precise oxidative stress regulation, with promising applications in photodynamic and antioxidant therapy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characteristics of the Emo-CDs. a) Schematic of the fabrication of the Emo-CDs. b) TEM and HRTEM images of Emo-CDs. c) Size distribution and d) XRD pattern of the Emo-CDs. e) Raman spectra of the Emo-CDs, D and G band are located in 1360 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1, respectively. f) FT-IR spectrum of Emo-CDs. g) Zeta potential diagrams of emodin and Emo-CDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of h) C 1s and i) N 1s of the Emo-CDs. j) Normalized UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of Emo-CDs in PBS. k) Excitation-emission 3D color map of Emo-CDs in PBS. l) Fluorescence lifetime fitting curve of the Emo-CDs. m) Fluorescence stability profiles of Emo-CDs over time under varying pH conditions. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

ROS generation or scavenging of Emo-CDs under WLED irradiation (100 mW cm−2) or dark conditions. a) The potential process diagram of photo-activated ROS generation. b) The total ROS detection using DCFH-DA as an indicator upon WLED irradiation in the presence of the Emo-CDs in PBS. c) ESR spectra of TMEP capturing 1O2 under different treatments. d) ESR spectra of DMPO capturing O2 •- under different treatments. e) UV–vis diffuse reflection spectrum of Emo-CDs. f) Tauc plot of Emo-CDs. g) Valence band XPS spectrum of Emo-CDs. h) Schematic illustrations on redox potential of Emo-CDs for type I ROS generation mechanism. i) ABTS radical scavenging ability of Emo-CDs. j) •OH scavenging ability of Emo-CDs. k) ESR spectra of DMPO capturing •OH signals at different concentrations of Emo-CDs. l) SOD-like activity of Emo-CDs (n = 3). m) ESR spectra of DMPO capturing O2 •- signals at different concentrations of Emo-CDs. n) Cyclic voltammogram of Emo-CDs showing their redox potential. o) The reduction potentials of SOD and Emo-CDs in the related reactions. p) Schematic diagram of ROS-scavenging capacity of Emo-CDs.

DFT calculations elucidate the photodynamic process and SOD-like properties. a) Molecular structure model a) DFT calculated HOMO and LUMO and computed energies. c) Energy profiles the S1 and T1 states of three molecular structure models. Gibbs free energy profiles of the SOD-like catalytic cycles in CDs: d) Free energy curve for the carbonyl-mediated SOD-like catalytic cycle e) Free energy curve for the cooperative hydroxyl-amino SOD-like catalytic cycle.

ROS generation of Emo-CDs under WLED irradiation (100 mW cm−2) and ROS scavenging under dark in vitro. a) CLSM images of 4T1 and HEK293T cells incubated with 40 μg/mL Emo-CDs. The blue represents nuclear staining, while the red indicates the emission from the Emo-CDs. Scale bar: 20 μm. b) After incubating 4T1 cells with Emo-CDs, different treatment conditions were applied to detect 1O2, O2 •-, and •OH within the cells. 1O2 was detected using SOSG, O2 •- was measured with DHE, and •OH was assessed using HPF. Emo-CDs protect HEK293T cells from oxidative stress induced by c) LPS, d) Fenton reagent, and e) H2O2. f) CLSM images showing DCFH-DA, DAF-FM DA, and Calcein-AM/PI, staining of HEK293T cells under different treatments. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Antitumor of Emo-CDs under WLED irradiation (100 mW cm−2) in vivo. a) Schematic diagram of anticancer treatment process in tumor-bearing mice. b) Digital images of the tumor in vitro after 14 days of different treatment. c) Tumor volume growth curves. d) Tumor tissue weight after 14 days of treatments. e) Changes in body weight of mice during the treatment period. f) H&E, TUNEL, Bcl-2, Ki67, and CD31 staining of tumors from different treatment groups after 14 days of treatment, scale bar: 200 μm. Data are presented as mean ± standard deviation (n = 5).

Antioxidant therapy effect of Emo-CDs on AKI mice. a) Schematic diagram of AKI mouse model establishment and treatment protocol. b) In vivo fluorescence imaging system showed the fluorescence changes of major organs after Emo-CDs injection in AKI mice at different time points. c) Fluorescence intensity graphs of major organs of AKI mice at different time intervals. Both AKI mice and the control group exhibited normal serum levels of BUN d) and CRE e). f) H&E staining of kidney tissues from mice in the control and AKI groups. The five-pointed star indicates the formation of casts. g) Kidney tissues were stained with dihydroethidine (DHE, red fluorescence) and DAPI (blue fluorescence). h) Survival rate of AKI mice treated with Emo-CDs and PBS. The levels of i) SOD, j) KIM-1, and k) HO-1 in kidney tissues. Data are presented as mean ± standard deviation (n = 4). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

In vivo biocompatibility of Emo-CDs. a) Evaluation of the in vivo toxicity of Emo-CDs to major organs (heart, liver, spleen, and lung) 24 h after intravenous injection. b) Evaluation of the renal in vivo toxicity of Emo-CDs 24 h after intravenous administration. c) Serum levels of the inflammatory factors IL-6 and TNF-α. d) Serum levels of liver function indicators AST and ALT. e) Serum levels of kidney function indicators:BUN and CRE. f-j) Blood parameters in normal mice (control group) and mice intravenously injected with Emo-CDs, 24 h after injection.
Preparation of CDs and their applications in PDT and renal protection against kidney injury.

head.ce:title: Emodin-derived red-emissive carbon dots: Light-driven ROS generation and antioxidant activities in non-illuminative regimes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) play dual roles in physiological processes and disease pathogenesis, making their precise regulation crucial for therapeutic applications. Herein, we report the rational design of red-emissive carbon dots (CDs) with light-switchable pro-oxidant/antioxidant bifunctionality for precision nanomedicine. Synthesized from emodin and urea via a solvothermal method, these N-doped CDs exhibit: (1) red emission (>650 nm) with enhanced tissue penetration, (2) efficient type I/II ROS generation under light irradiation, (3) effective ROS scavenging in darkness , and superior water solubility and biocompatibility for in vivo applications. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations confirm that N-doping optimizes the spectral properties of Emo-CDs and promotes the generation of ROS. The unique graphene-like core with abundant surface functional groups enables reversible switching between oxidative and antioxidative states under dark conditions. This work establishes a new paradigm for developing intelligent theranostic agents capable of precise oxidative stress regulation, with promising applications in photodynamic and antioxidant therapy.

Reactive oxygen species (ROS) play vital roles in cellular functions, but their overproduction causes oxidative stress, leading to diseases like cancer and neurodegeneration. CDs, especially red-emitting ones, offer dual functionality by generating ROS under light and scavenging ROS in darkness, making them promising for photodynamic therapy, antioxidant therapy, and disease management.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: ROS are a diverse group of highly reactive oxygen-containing molecules, including hydroxyl radicals (•OH), singlet oxygen (1O2), superoxide anions (O2 •-), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), and other related species [1]. These molecules play essential roles in cellular and tissue functions [2–4], such as signal transduction [5,6], immune defense [7–9], and cell proliferation [10,11]. However, overproduced ROS can induce oxidative stress, leading to cellular damage [12,13], aging [14,15], and the development of various diseases [16], including cancer [17], neurodegenerative disorders [18], and cardiovascular conditions [19]. Therefore, the precise regulation of ROS production and timely clearance has become a critical challenge in advancing therapeutic strategies and improving medical treatments. For this purpose, antioxidants are commonly used to neutralize excess ROS, whereas, innovative therapies like photodynamic therapy (PDT) are exploited as photosensitizers to selectively generate ROS, specifically for targeting and destroying cancer cells [20,21].

In recent years, small-sized quantum dots, including metal quantum dots, semiconductor quantum dots, and CDs, have garnered significant attention for their ability to regulate intracellular ROS [22]. These quantum dots exhibit unique optical and electronic properties, making them highly promising for cellular biology research and therapeutic applications [23–28], particularly in the treatment of various diseases [29]. Among them, CDs stand out due to their superior biocompatibility [30], low toxicity [31], cost-effectiveness [32], environmental friendliness [33], and tenability [34]. One intriguing feature of CDs is their photoluminescence in solution, which can be quenched by electron acceptor or donor molecules [35–37]. Moreover, the graphene-like structure of CDs, along with their surface-rich hydrophilic functional groups such as amine (-NH2), hydroxyl (-OH), and carbonyl (-CO), significantly enhances their ability to scavenge ROS [38]. This indicates that CDs have the unique potential to simultaneously exhibit pro-oxidant properties under irradiation and antioxidant activities in darkness, which opens up new avenues for therapeutic interventions [39].

In biomedical applications, red-emitting CDs [40,41] have garnered significant attention due to their low biological toxicity and the strong tissue penetration of red light [42,43]. These properties make them highly suitable for a wide range of applications, including PDT [44,45], antioxidant therapy [45,46], and diagnostic imaging [47]. For example, citric acid-derived red-emitting CDs have been widely used in biological imaging and PDT [48–50], while glutathione-based red-emitting CDs have been explored for biological imaging and antioxidant therapy to reduce tissue damage [38,51]. Additionally, red-emitting CDs have been developed for various applications such as biosensing [52], in vivo diagnostics [53], therapies [54], and light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [55]. Although significant progress has been made in optimizing the luminescent properties of CDs and regulating their pro-oxidant and antioxidant activities, studies on red-emitting CDs with the dual function of generating ROS under irradiation and scavenging ROS in the dark remain limited. While graphene quantum dots have demonstrated good pro-oxidant activity under photo-irradiation and antioxidant properties under dark conditions, their relatively short excitation and emission wavelengths and dependence on short-wavelength excitation light significantly restrict their clinical potential (Table S1) [56–60]. Moreover, most reported PDT-active CDs primarily produce ROS via a type II mechanism, which is oxygen-dependent and thus exhibits limited efficacy against hypoxic (oxygen-deficient) tumors. Although a few CDs with mixed type I and type II mechanisms have been reported, their poor water solubility remains a major obstacle for in vivo applications (Table S2). Notably, currently reported ROS-scavenging CDs generally suffer from short excitation/emission wavelengths, which remains a critical bottleneck hindering their clinical application (Table S3). Consequently, designing red-emitting CDs with light/dark-triggered switching capability between pro-oxidant (featuring both type I and type II ROS generation) and antioxidant activities presents a crucial research challenge.

Herein, this study develops a red-emitting CDs (Emo-CDs) capable of generating ROS under irradiation and scavenging ROS under dark conditions. Emodin serves as an excellent carbon precursor for CDs synthesis owing to its dual functional characteristics: (1) the polyphenol moieties promote hydroxyl group enrichment on the CD surface, and (2) the native extended conjugation system enhances the π-electron delocalization in the carbon core, resulting in significant bathochromic shifts in optical properties. Additionally, urea was employed as an innovative nitrogen dopant, concurrently enabling wavelength red-shifting through N-doping and enhancing surface amino-group density on the CDs. Emo-CDs were successfully synthesized via a solvothermal strategy using emodin-urea dual precursors in ethanol medium. N-doping reduces the bandgap of the CDs, causing a redshift in both absorption and emission wavelengths. DFT calculations further indicate that N-doping effectively narrows the bandgap and enhances the photodynamic process, leading to ROS generation under irradiation. Additionally, emodin, a polyphenolic compound, undergoes carbonization with urea, resulting in a graphene-like surface rich in functional groups such as –OH, –NH2, and CO. These functional groups play a key role in the scavenging of ROS. As a result, the CDs can both generate and effectively remove ROS. The aim of this study is to develop and evaluate this novel red-emitting CDs, with the expectation of providing new strategies for nanomedicine, particularly in cancer treatment and antioxidant therapy (Scheme 1 ).

Emodin, urea, ferrous sulfate heptahydrate, 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF), 2,7-dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA) were purchased from Bide Company (Shanghai, China). Hydroxyphenyl Fluorescein (HPF) was purchased from Cayman Chemical. Dihydrorhodamine 123 (DHR123) was obtained from MREDA company (Beijing, China) and dihydroethidium (DHE), lipopolysaccharides (LPS), diaminofluorescein-FM diacetate (DAF-FM DA), singlet oxygen sensor green (SOSG), and calcein-AM/PI kits, Cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) and methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) assay kit were obtained from Beyotime Biotechnology Company (Beijing, China). Cell apoptosis detection kit was purchased from Dalian Meilun Biotechnology Co., Ltd.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images were obtained from a JEM-F200. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were acquired from a Nicolet 5700 FT-IR spectrometer. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded on a U-3900 UV–vis spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on AXIS Supra (Kratos, England). Fluorescence emission spectra were performed on a Horiba Fluorolog-3 spectrophotometer. Raman spectra were acquired from Horiba LabRAM HR spectrometer. Cell images were captured on a ZEISS confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) (LSM880 Airyscan with STEDYCON, Germany). ESR spectra were recorded on a EMXplus (BrukerBioSpin, Germany) spectrometer. In vivo fluorescence images were collected by an IVIS Spectrum imaging system (PerkinElmer IVIS).

First, 0.2 g of Emodin and 0.5 g of urea were added to 25 mL of ethanol for 10 min by ultrasound, and the mixed solution was placed in a 100 mL Teflon-autoclave. The autoclave was placed in blast drying oven at 180 °C for 8 h. When the autoclave temperature decreased to 25 °C, the resulting red solution was centrifuged at 9000 r/min for 15 min, the supernatant was collected and then filtered via 0.22 μm ultrafiltration. The supernatant was collected and subsequently purified through silica gel column chromatography, resulting in the isolation of a red solid, which was named Emo-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Total ROS generation characterization of Emo-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The ROS generation capacity of Emo-CDs was evaluated using DCFH-DA as a fluorescent probe. The DCFH-DA stock solution was prepared by incubating 2 mL of 1 mM DCFH-DA (in ethanol) with 8 mL of 10 mM NaOH aqueous solution for 30 min at room temperature, followed by neutralization with 40 mL of PBS buffer (pH 7.4) to yield a final concentration of 20 μM. For the assay, 5 μL of Emo-CDs stock solution (4 mg mL−1 in DMSO) was mixed with 2995 μL of the prepared DCFH solution. The reaction mixture was then exposed to white LED light for varying time intervals. The fluorescence signal of DCFH was monitored to indicate the ROS generation with the excitation wavelength of 488 nm and the emission wavelength of 530 nm.

DPBF was used as a specific probe to detect 1O2 species, as it reacts with 1O2 and exhibits a reduction in absorbance at 410 nm. Briefly, a solution containing 10 μg/mL Emo-CDs and 50 μM DPBF in PBS was irradiated with white LED light for different time intervals. After irradiation, the reduction in absorbance at 410 nm was recorded using UV–Vis spectroscopy to evaluate 1O2 production.

The generation of O2 •- by Emo-CDs was characterized using DHR123 as a specific fluorescence probe. DHR123 reacts with O2 •- species and exhibits strong green fluorescence emission at 527 nm. Briefly, a solution containing 20 μg/mL Emo-CDs and 10 μM DHR123 in PBS was irradiated with white LED light for varying time intervals. The fluorescence signal was then monitored to assess O2 •- generation, with excitation at 488 nm and emission at 527 nm.

The generation of •OH by Emo-CDs was characterized using HPF as a specific fluorescence probe. HPF reacts with •OH species and exhibits strong green fluorescence emission at 515 nm. Briefly, a solution containing 20 μg/mL Emo-CDs and 20 μM HPF in PBS was irradiated with white LED light for varying time intervals. The fluorescence signal was then monitored to assess •OH generation, with excitation at 488 nm and emission at 515 nm.

The production of 1O2 was detected by electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy, using 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) as a spin-trap agent. TEMP captures 1O2, forming the stable nitroxide radical TEMPO. The absorbance of the Emo-CDs solution in DMF at 532 nm was adjusted to approximately 0.2 OD. Then, 50 μL of 4 mM TEMP was added to the Emo-CDs solution. The mixture was irradiated with white LED light for either 5 or 7 min. ESR spectra of the spin adduct were monitored in the range of 3460–3560 G, with background interference collected from the sample prior to irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of O2 •- production by ESR
ce:section.ce:para: ESR spectroscopy was employed to monitor O2 •- generation using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) as a spin-trap agent. Upon O2 •- production, it reacts with DMPO to form the DMPO-OOH adduct. The absorbance of the Emo-CDs solution in water at 532 nm was adjusted to approximately 0.2 OD. Then, 10 μL of 0.2 M DMPO was added to the Emo-CDs solution. The mixture was irradiated with white LED light for either 5 or 10 min. ESR spectra of the spin adduct were monitored in the range of 3460–3560 G, with background interference collected from the sample before irradiation.

CV measurements were performed on an electrochemical analyzer (CHI 660E, CHI Instrument). Emo-CDs film sprayed on a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) served as the working electrode. An Ag/AgCl (KCl-saturated) and a platinum wire were used as the reference electrode and the counter electrode, respectively. CV experiments were carried out after purging the HEPES buffer solution (0.1 m, pH 7.4) with nitrogen for 30 min at room temperature. The scan rate was 10 mV s−1. Normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) potential was calculated by the equation: E (NHE) = E (Ag/AgCl) + 0.197 V.

4T1 and HEK293T cells were purchased from the Institute of Cell Biology (Shanghai, China). Cells were propagated in cell culture flask at 37 °C under humidified 5 % CO2 atmosphere. Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM, GIBCO/Invitrogen, Camarillo, CA, USA) was supplemented with 1 % penicillin-streptomycin (10,000 U mL−1 penicillin, and 10 mg mL−1 streptomycin, Solarbio life science, Beijing, China) and 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS, Biological Industry, Kibbutz Beit Haemek, Israel).

The generation of 1O2 in vitro was detected using SOSG as a fluorescence probe. Briefly, 4T1 cells were seeded in 35 mm confocal dishes and incubated for 24 h. Afterward, the cells were treated with Emo-CDs (20 μg/mL) for 4 h. The cells were then fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde for 15 min. Following three washes with PBS, the cells were incubated with a 10 μM SOSG probe for 20 min. The cells were subsequently irradiated with white LED light for 10 min. Finally, 1O2 generation was imaged using a confocal laser scanning microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of O2 •- generation in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: The generation of O2 •- in vitro was detected using DHE as a fluorescence probe. Briefly, 4T1 cells were seeded in 35 mm confocal dishes and incubated for 24 h. After incubation with Emo-CDs (20 μg/mL) for 4 h, the cells were treated with fresh medium containing 20 μM DHE and incubated for an additional 30 min. The cells were then irradiated with white LED light for 10 min. Finally, O2 •- generation was imaged using a confocal laser scanning microscope.

The generation of •OH in vitro was detected using HPF as a fluorescence probe. Briefly, 4T1 cells were seeded in 35 mm confocal dishes and incubated for 24 h. After incubation with Emo-CDs (20 μg/mL) for 4 h, the cells were treated with fresh medium containing 20 μM HPF and incubated for an additional 30 min. The cells were then irradiated with white LED light for 10 min. Finally, •OH generation was imaged using a confocal laser scanning microscope.

The cytotoxic effects of Emo-CDs were evaluated using the CCK-8 assay. Briefly, 4T1 and HEK293T were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 1 × 105 cells per well in complete growth medium and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C under 5 % CO2. The culture medium was then replaced with fresh medium containing various concentrations of Emo-CDs (0, 25, 50, 75, 100, 150, and 200 μg/mL), followed by another 24 h incubation. After treatment, cells were gently washed once with sterile PBS and incubated with 100 μL fresh medium plus 10 μL CCK-8 solution for 2 h at 37 °C in the dark. The absorbance at 450 nm was measured using a microplate reader (Thermo Varioskan Flash, USA). Cells treated without Emo-CDs (0 μg/mL) served as the control group.

4T1 cells were seeded in a 96-well plate and cultured overnight. The following day, the cells were treated with Emo-CDs (40 μg/mL) for 4 h at 37 °C. After treatment, the cells were irradiated with white LED light for varying time intervals and incubated for an additional 24 h. Cell viability was assessed using a standard MTT assay, Cells treated without irradiation served as the control group. To visually distinguish live (green fluorescence) and dead (red fluorescence) cells, the 4T1 cells were further stained with Calcein-AM/PI.

Approximately 2 × 105 4T1 cells in 2 mL of the corresponding medium were seeded in a 6-well plate and incubated overnight at 37 °C in a humidified 5 % CO2 atmosphere. The cells were then treated with Emo-CDs under different conditions: i) control, ii) white LED light alone, iii) Emo-CDs alone without irradiation, and iv) Emo-CDs + white LED light. After these treatments, the cells were incubated for an additional 24 h. The cells were then washed twice with PBS and harvested using 0.25 % trypsin-EDTA (Invitrogen, 0.5 mL) for 5 min. Trypsin activity was quenched with a serum-containing medium (0.5 mL), and the cell suspension was centrifuged at 1500 rpm for 3 min at room temperature. The subsequent steps were performed according to the instructions provided with the cell apoptosis detection kit.

Approximately 1 × 105 HEK293T cells in 2 mL of the corresponding medium were seeded in a 6-well plate and incubated overnight at 37 °C in a humidified 5 % CO2 atmosphere. The cells were then treated with Emo-CDs under different conditions: i) PBS, ii) Emo-CDs, iii) H2O2, and iv) Emo-CDs + H2O2. After these treatments, the cells were incubated for an additional 24 h. The cells were then washed twice with PBS and harvested using 0.25 % trypsin-EDTA (Invitrogen, 0.5 mL) for 5 min. Trypsin activity was quenched with a serum-containing medium (0.5 mL), and the cell suspension was centrifuged at 1500 rpm for 3 min at room temperature. The subsequent steps were performed according to the instructions provided with the cell apoptosis detection kit.

Female BALB/c nude mice (6–8 weeks old) were obtained from Beijing HFK Bioscience Technology Co., Ltd. The mice were maintained under pathogen-free conditions with ad libitum access to food and water. 4T1 cells (5 × 105 cells) were subcutaneously inoculated into the mice's backs. Once the tumors reached a size of 60–80 mm3, Emo-CDs were intratumoral injection. In vivo fluorescence imaging was conducted before and after injection at various time intervals up to 12 h (excitation wavelength = 532 nm, emission wavelength ≥650 nm). Fluorescence signals from the harvested tumors and organs were recorded using the IVIS Spectrum system.

Animals and Tumor Model: Female BALB/c mice (6–8 weeks old) were obtained from Beijing HFK Bioscience Technology Co., Ltd. To establish a tumor model, 4T1 cells (5 × 105) were inoculated at the right axillary fossa of the mice. 4T1 tumor-bearing mice were used for in vivo experiments when the tumor volume reached 60–80 mm3. In vivo Anticancer Performance: twenty 4T1 tumor-bearing female BALB/c mice were randomly divided into four groups (n = 5 per group) as follows: i) control, ii) White LED alone, iii) Emo-CDs without white LED, and iv) Emo-CDs + white LED. At 1 h post-injection, the tumor sites were irradiated with white LED for 15 min (White light irradiation was performed at a power density of 40 mW cm−2 for 15 min, and Emo-CDs were administered at a dose of 50 μL with a concentration of 200 μg/mL). The mice received intratumoral injections every other day for a total of three administrations, with body weight and tumor volume measured and recorded at each time point. The tumor volume (mm3) was obtained according to V = lw2/2, in which w and l were the width and length of the tumor, respectively. The tumor-bearing mice were sacrificed after 14 days of different treatments. Next, the tumor tissues were washed three times with PBS and fixed with 10 % neutral buffered formalin solution. After paraffin embedding, the tissues were sectioned for histological analysis. H&E staining, TUNEL assay, and immunohistochemical staining for Bcl-2, Ki67, and CD31 were performed, and the tumor sections were observed under a light microscope for pathological evaluation.

The ability of Emo-CDs to scavenge O2 •- was investigated using the WST-1 assay kit, according to the manufacturer's instructions. Briefly, O2 •- was generated through the oxidation of xanthine by xanthine oxidase (XOD). The amount of formazan formed is directly related to the amount of O2 •- generated in the reaction system. To verify the O2 •- scavenging ability of Emo-CDs, different concentrations (0, 2.5, 5, 7.5, 10, 20, 40, 60, and 80 μg/mL) of Emo-CDs dispersion were mixed with WST-1, xanthine, and XOD, and the changes in the absorbance of water-soluble formazan at 450 nm were measured using a microplate reader (n = 3 for each group).

The synthesized Emo-CDs were used to scavenge •OH radicals by employing ESR spectroscopy at room temperature. The •OH generation was initiated using Fenton-like reagents and trapped as the spin adduct DMPO/•OH. Briefly, ESR measurement was conducted by incubating FeSO4 with H2O2 aqueous solution, followed by the addition of Emo-CDs and DMPO. The ESR spectra were recorded after 2 min of incubation. Additionally, the TMB chromogenic method was used to evaluate the •OH scavenging activity. •OH was generated via the classical Fenton reaction between H2O2 and Fe2+, which converts TMB into oxidized TMB (oxTMB) with a characteristic absorption at 652 nm. Therefore, the concentration of remaining •OH was determined by monitoring the absorption of oxTMB at 652 nm. In detail, the working test solutions were prepared in the dark, containing 250 μM TMB, 2 mM H2O2, 1 mM FeSO4, and different concentrations of Emo-CDs (40–120 μg/mL) in HAc/NaAc buffer (0.5 M, pH 4.5), and left to stand for 5 min. The absorbance peak at 652 nm of the solution was then monitored using UV–vis spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 2,2′-azinobis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid (ABTS) radical scavenging activity of Emo-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, ABTS radicals were generated by reacting a 7 mM ABTS stock solution with 2.45 mM potassium persulfate in the dark for 16 h. The resulting ABTS radical solution was then diluted with PBS to achieve the appropriate absorbance at 734 nm. Two milliliters of Emo-CDs solutions (2.5–30 μg/mL) were mixed with 2 mL of the ABTS solution and incubated in the dark for 5 min. The absorbance at 734 nm was then measured using UV–vis spectroscopy. The ABTS radical scavenging abilities were calculated as follows: ABTS scavenging ratio (%) = ((Acontrol – A sample)/Acontrol) × 100, where Acontrol is the absorbance of the standard solution without any radical scavengers, and Asample is the absorbance after the reaction with the radical scavengers, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Emo-CDs protects cells from oxidative damages
ce:section.ce:para: HEK293T cells were cultured in a 48-well cell culture plate for 24 h at a density of 60–80 %. Then, the medium was replaced with fresh medium containing Emo-CDs at different concentrations, and the cells were incubated for 4 h. Subsequently, the cells were treated under different conditions, including LPS, Fenton reagent, and H2O2 treatments. For the LPS treatment, 10 μg/mL of LPS was co-cultured with the cells for 24 h. The H2O2 treatment involved 500 μM H2O2 for 4 h. The Fenton reagent, consisting of 200 μM H2O2 and 40 μM Fe2+, was added to the cell culture medium and incubated for 4 h. After all treatments, the cells were incubated at 37 °C and 5 % CO2 in a constant temperature incubator. After the treatments, cell viability was assessed using the CCK-8 viability assay kit. The culture medium was replaced with serum-free medium containing CCK-8 reagent (10:1), and the cells were co-cultured for 2 h. Finally, cell viability was determined using a SpectraMax iD5 reader (n = 3 for each group).

The generation of intracellular ROS was assessed using the DCFH-DA probe. Intracellular ROS can oxidize DCFH into fluorescent DCF, which serves as an indicator of ROS levels. RNS generation was detected using the DAF-FM DA probe. Cells were pre-treated with H2O2 to induce damage, and then incubated with 10 μM DCFH-DA or DAF-FM DA at 37 °C for 30 min. After incubation, the cells were washed three times with PBS to remove excess dye. Finally, the treated cells were imaged using confocal laser scanning microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Acute kidney injury (AKI) models in mice
ce:section.ce:para: The glycerol-induced AKI model was established according to a previously reported protocol [61]. Briefly, 8–10-week-old female BALB/c mice weighing 20–25g were deprived of water for 17 h, while having free access to food. After the water deprivation, 50 % glycerol was intramuscularly injected into both hind limbs of the mice at a dose of 8 mL/kg. Following the injection, the mice were allowed free access to water and food. Symptoms of AKI, such as reduced activity and decreased urine output, could be observed a few hours after glycerol injection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo therapeutic effect of Emo-CDs in the mouse AKI model
ce:section.ce:para: BALB/c mice with established AKI model were randomly divided into two groups (n = 4): one group received PBS injection (AKI group), and the other group was treated with Emo-CDs at a dosage of 20 mg/kg. After 24 h post-injection, the mice were sacrificed, and blood samples were collected to detect blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and creatinine (CRE) levels. The left kidneys were harvested and homogenized for detection of renal biomarkers. The level of SOD in the kidneys was measured using an SOD assay kit (Sigma-Aldrich, USA). The expression levels of two important kidney injury biomarkers, heme oxygenase-1 (HO-1) and kidney injury molecule-1 (KIM-1), were detected using HO-1 (TQ9935, Techisun) and KIM-1 (TQ9770, Techisun) ELISA kits, respectively. The right kidneys were cut into two equal sections. One section was fixed with 4 % neutral buffered formalin and embedded in paraffin for H&E staining. The other section was frozen and embedded in optimal cutting temperature compound and sectioned at −20 °C to a thickness of approximately 5 μm. The frozen kidney tissue sections were stained with DAPI and DHE at 37 °C for 30 min, gently washed three times with PBS to remove excess dye, and then imaged under a fluorescence microscope to qualitatively detect the ROS levels in the renal tissues.

Results were shown as the mean value ± standard error of measurements (SEM), unless otherwise noted. Graphpad Prism 9 was used for the statistical analyses, which were followed by one-way ANOVA with Tukey's multiple comparisons test (∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001, and ∗∗∗∗p < 0.0001, ns p > 0.05).

All animal experiments in this study were carried out after the approval of the Medical Ethics Committee of Sichuan University, adhering to both institutional and NIH guidelines for the care and use of research animals (approval number KS2022864).

The Emo-CDs were synthesized using emodin as the carbon source and urea as the nitrogen source (Fig. 1 a). The mixture was heated to 180 °C in ethanol for 8 h, resulting in a deep red solution. The solution was then centrifuged at 9000 r/min, and the supernatant was collected and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The filtered liquid was further purified by silica gel column chromatography, using a MeOH: DCM (1:10, v/v) mixture for elution. Finally, the red solid powder, corresponding to Emo-CDs, was obtained under reduced pressure distillation.

The morphology and diameter of Emo-CDs were characterized using TEM and atomic force microscopy (AFM). TEM images reveals that the synthesized CDs exhibit excellent dispersibility and a quasi-spherical morphology (Fig. 1b), with an average particle size of 1.75 nm (Fig. 1c). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis confirmed the hydrodynamic diameter of Emo-CDs as 1.78 nm (Fig. S1), showing close agreement with TEM measurements. The HR-TEM images displayed a distinct lattice fringe structure within the Emo-CDs, with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) facet of graphite (Fig. 1b, insert image). Additionally, AFM images further confirmed the good dispersion and uniform spherical morphology of the Emo-CDs (Fig. S2), with an average height matching that observed in the TEM images.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) profile of Emo-CDs (Fig. 1d) exhibits characteristic diffraction features, including a prominent peak at 25° corresponding to the (002) plane with d-spacing of 0.34 nm, and a secondary reflection at 41° indicative of a 0.21 nm interlayer distance. The Raman spectrum of Emo-CDs exhibits two major peaks at 1360 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1, corresponding to the D and G bands in the carbon core structure, respectively (Fig. 1e). These peaks represent structural defects in the CDs (D band) and the graphene-like structure (G band).

FT-IR, XPS, and Raman spectroscopy were employed to characterize the functional groups, chemical composition, and surface structure of the Emo-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1f, the FT-IR spectrum exhibits two prominent peaks at 3467 cm−1 and 3325 cm−1, which correspond to the stretching vibrations of –OH and –NH2 groups in the Emo-CDs, respectively. These peaks suggest that urea participates in the synthesis of Emo-CDs and the CDs surface is rich in –OH and –NH2 groups. Three distinct peaks are obviously observed in the spectrum at 1687 cm−1, 1621 cm−1, and 1467 cm−1, which correspond to the stretching vibrations of the CO, (imide) CN, and ether (C–O) bonds. In addition, the stretching vibrations of aromatic C–H and C–N bonds are observed at 2853 cm−1and 1175 cm−1, respectively. Zeta potential measurements in Fig. 1g confirm successful surface functionalization, with emodin-urea derived Emo-CDs showing a positive charge due to amino group incorporation, unlike the negative charge of pristine emodin. The XPS spectrum reveals the chemical composition of the Emo-CDs, which primarily includes C, N, and O elements. The weight percentages of these elements are 74.4 %, 4.6 %, and 21.1 %, respectively (Fig. S3). The presence of N atom further confirms the role of urea in the formation of the Emo-CDs structure, which is consistent with the FT-IR spectroscopy analysis. High-resolution XPS spectra show that the three major peaks in the C 1s region are located at 284.8 eV, 286.2 eV, and 289.2 eV, corresponding to the CC, C–O/C–N, and CO bonds, respectively (Fig. 1h). The N 1s high-resolution XPS spectra also display three peaks at 396.3 eV, 397.2 eV, and 399.8 eV, which are attributed to nitrogen atoms in pyrrolic N, graphitic N, and pyridinic N structure, respectively (Fig. 1i). These high-resolution XPS results further elucidate the bonding modes of carbon and nitrogen elements within the CDs structure. These characterization results show that the structure and chemical composition of the CDs are influenced by urea and are rich in various hydrophilic functional groups.

The photophysical properties of Emo-CDs were investigated using UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of Emo-CDs in PBS are shown in Fig. 1j. In the visible region, the absorption spectrum of Emo-CDs is primarily concentrated between 500 and 600 nm, with a maximum absorption peak at 540 nm and a tail extending to 712 nm. The UV–Vis spectrum of Emo-CDs clearly exhibits four distinct characteristic absorption peaks at 269 nm, 313 nm, 387 nm, and 540 nm (Fig. S4). Specifically, the absorption peaks at 269 nm and 540 nm can be attributed to the π→π∗ electronic transitions of CC bonds and the extended conjugated system in the carbon core, which is consistent with the aromatic carbon framework characteristics of typical CDs. The absorption peaks at 313 nm and 387 nm originate from the n→π∗ electronic transitions of CO/CN and other oxygen/nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of the Emo-CDs, indicating that the synthesized Emo-CDs possess abundant surface functional group modifications. The fluorescence emission spectrum is mainly between 600 and 700 nm, with the maximum emission peak at 675 nm and the emission tail extending to 785 nm. The excitation-emission 3D color map of Emo-CDs reveals that the optimal excitation wavelength range is between 475 and 575 nm (Fig. 1k). The fluorescence decay curve shown in Fig. 1l indicates an average lifetime of 6.50 ns. Additionally, we systematically evaluated the fluorescence stability of Emo-CDs under varying pH conditions over time (Fig. 1m). The results demonstrate that Emo-CDs exhibit optimal fluorescence emission intensity under neutral to weakly alkaline conditions (pH 7–8). Notably, the Emo-CDs maintained excellent photostability throughout the testing period, with negligible fluorescence attenuation observed over consecutive days of monitoring (Fig. S5). Furthermore, under ambient light conditions, the Emo-CDs showed remarkable stability in aqueous media containing different salt concentrations, as evidenced by minimal changes in physical appearance (color and dispersion state) during long-term storage (Fig. S6). These results demonstrate that Emo-CDs exhibit broad visible light absorption, strong red fluorescence emission and excellent stability in aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ROS generation or scavenging by Emo-CDs under light irradiation or dark conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The potential process diagram of photo-activated ROS generation is schemed in Fig. 2 a. Under light irradiation, the photosensitizer absorbs photons and reaches first excited state (S1), followed by intersystem crossing (ISC) to its first triplet excited state (T1). In this excited state, the photosensitizer can transfer energy to O2 in the environment, generating 1O2 and initiating a type II photodynamic process. Alternatively, it can undergo electron transfer to produce ROS such as O2 •−and •OH, leading to a type I photodynamic process (Fig. 2a). Initially, DCFH-DA as a specific fluorescent probe to systematically investigate the ROS generation capability of Emo-CDs under a white LED (WLED). As shown in Fig. 2b, the DCF fluorescence intensity exhibited a continuous

enhancement with prolonged irradiation time, unequivocally demonstrating the significant ROS production capacity of Emo-CDs under photoexcitation conditions. To investigate the types of ROS generated by Emo-CDs under light irradiation, we used three specific ROS probes: DPBF, DHR123, and HPF, to detect 1O2, O2 •-, and •OH, respectively. As depicted in Fig. S7, time-dependent DPBF degradation assays were performed to evaluate 1O2 generation capability of Emo-CDs under WLED irradiation. The characteristic DPBF absorption at 410 nm exhibited a significant decrease in the presence of photoexcited Emo-CDs, while control experiments (without Emo-CDs and light exposure) showed negligible changes, indicating that Emo-CDs can produce 1O2 under light irradiation. Additionally, the absorption of DPBF dropped sharply within 10 s of light irradiation, further proving the efficient generation of 1O2 by Emo-CDs and the occurrence of a type II photodynamic process. DHR123 and HPF are specific fluorescence probes for O2 •-, and •OH, respectively. When DHR123 and HPF react with O2 •-, and •OH, their maximum fluorescence emission increases with peaks at 527 nm and 515 nm, respectively. As shown in Figs. S8 and S9, upon adding Emo-CDs to these probes and irradiating with WLED, the fluorescence emission intensity of both DHR123 and HPF gradually increased with the prolongation of irradiation time. This demonstrates that Emo-CDs can generate O2 •-, and •OH, confirming the occurrence of the type I photodynamic process. To further confirm that Emo-CDs can simultaneously generate both type I and type II photodynamic reactions under WLED irradiation, we utilized ESR spectroscopy to detect 1O2 and O2 •- respectively. DMPO and TEMP were used to capture O2 •- and 1O2, respectively. Under dark conditions, no ROS signals were detected. However, after 5 or 7 min of irradiation, signals corresponding to singlet 1O2 and O2 •- were captured, further confirming that Emo-CDs can generate both type I and type II ROS under WLED illumination (Fig. 2c and d).

Due to the presence of pyrrole nitrogen groups in the Emo-CDs structure, they are capable of generating ROS under WLED irradiation [62], including 1O2, O2 •-, and •OH. Furthermore, we investigated the underlying catalytic mechanism of Emo-CDs in type I photodynamic process. As shown in Fig. 2e, UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra demonstrate that Emo-CDs exhibit broad absorption in the visible light range. Tauc plot analysis reveals that the bandgap (Eg) of Emo-CDs is 1.63 eV (Fig. 2f), while the XPS spectrum shows that the valence band (E VB) of Emo-CDs is located at 0.98 eV (Fig. 2g). Therefore, the conduction band (E CB) of Emo-CDs can be calculated to be - 0.65 eV, according to the equation E g = E VB - E CB. This value is more negative than the energy level from O2 to O2 •- (E(O2/O2 •-) = - 0.23 eV) [63], enabling Emo-CDs to effectively catalyze the reduction of O2 to generate O2 •−process (Fig. 2h).

Considering the abundant –OH, –NH2, and CO groups in the graphene-like structure of Emo-CDs, we investigated their ability to scavenge ROS under dark conditions [64]. we investigated the ability of Emo-CDs to scavenge •OH and other radicals. ABTS generates a green ABTS radical in the presence of potassium persulfate, which has a maximum absorption at 734 nm. When the radical is scavenged, the green color fades. Therefore, Emo-CDs were added to the pre-activated ABTS radical solution, and their antioxidant activity was assessed by monitoring the absorption change at 734 nm. As shown in Fig. 2i and S10, with increasing concentrations of Emo-CDs, the ABTS radical was effectively scavenged, and the absorbance gradually decreased. At a concentration of 40 μg/mL, the scavenging rate exceeded 90 %. Additionally, we further investigated the ability of Emo-CDs to scavenge •OH and other radicals. Firstly, •OH was generated using the Fenton reaction, and 3,5,3′,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was used as an indicator to detect •OH. TMB reacts with•OH to form oxidized TMB, accompanied by an increase in absorption at 652 nm. When different concentrations of Emo-CDs were added, they effectively scavenged •OH, thereby inhibiting the oxidation of TMB (Fig. 2j and S11). ESR spectroscopy further demonstrated that Emo-CDs could scavenge •OH. As shown in Fig. 2k, the ESR signals of •OH were significantly suppressed after adding different concentrations of Emo-CDs. The total antioxidant capacity (TAC), a key parameter for evaluating antioxidant potential, is conventionally reported in ascorbic acid (vitamin C) equivalents (Fig. S10). Our findings reveal a positive correlation between TAC values and Emo-CDs concentration, with a calculated IC50 value of 22 μg/mL.

O2 •- are essential for maintaining ROS balance within cells, however, excessive levels can trigger oxidative stress. SOD catalyzes the conversion of O2 •- into H2O2 and O2, effectively mitigating oxidative stressby removing excess O2 •-. We then utilized (2-(4-iodophenyl)-3-(4-nitrophenyl)-5-(2,4-disulfonic acid phenyl)-tetrazolium sodium salt) as an indicator (WST-1), which reacts with O2 •- to produce a water-soluble dye, WST-1 formazan, characterized by its absorption peak at 450 nm, to prove the ROS scavenging property. SOD mimetics preferentially scavenge O2 •-, thereby inhibiting the formation of formazan. As shown in Fig. 2l, the scavenging efficiency of O2 •- significantly increased with rising concentrations of Emo-CDs. Notably, at a concentration of 80 μg/mL, the inhibition rate exceeded 90 %, demonstrating the SOD-like activity of Emo CDs. ESR analysis demonstrated concentration-dependent O2 •- scavenging by Emo-CDs, showing progressive signal attenuation that reached near-complete quenching at 100 μg/mL (Fig. 2m). To elucidate the SOD-like mechanism, cyclic voltammetry (CV) was employed to measure their redox potential (Fig. 2n). Thermodynamically, the dismutation of O2 •- involves two half-reactions (oxidation and reduction of O2 •-) with reduction potentials of E(O2/O2 •-) = 0.91 eV and E(O2 •-/H2O2) = - 0.18 eV, respectively. The results revealed that the redox potential of Emo-CDs is 0.21 eV (Fig. 2o), further confirming their ability to effectively catalyze the dismutation of O2 •-. Therefore, these results indicate that Emo-CDs exhibit SOD-like activity, and can effectively scavenge •OH and ABTS radicals in the absence of light (Fig. 2p).

ce:section.ce:section-title: DFT calculations reveal photodynamic processes and SOD properties
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate that nitrogen doping can effectively reduce the bandgap of CDs and promote photodynamic processes, we constructed molecular models of carbon cores with and without nitrogen doping, based on sp2 hybridized carbon atoms and molecular structure derived from the emodin. These models are named O-CDs and O,N-CDs, representing the structures of non-doped and nitrogen-doped CDs, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3 a. Time-dependent DFT was used to evaluate their optical properties and orbital energies. It is important to note that the relative comparison between the CD models helps identify trends and provides an explanation for the observed results. The HOMO and LUMO orbital energies of Emodin, O-CDs, and O,N-CDs were found to be 3.40 eV, 2.58 eV, and 1.83 eV, respectively (Fig. 3b). The results clearly indicate that nitrogen doping effectively reduces the orbital gap, enabling the CDs to exhibit red fluorescence emission. Furthermore, the energy differences between S1 and T1 for the three structures were 0.46 eV, 0.69 eV, and 0.59 eV, respectively (Fig. 3c). By comparing the differences between O-CDs and O,N-CDs, it was found that nitrogen doping not only reduces the bandgap but also promotes the photodynamic process. Therefore, these results suggest that Emo-CDs, under WLED illumination, can efficiently generate type I and type II ROS, while exhibiting excellent red emission properties.

The SOD activity serves as a crucial factor determining the ROS scavenging capability of CDs. we innovatively employed DFT calculations to construct a perylene-fused molecular structure model for simulating the SOD catalytic process of CDs. Notably, comparative analysis of different computational models enabled precise identification of catalytic trends and provided rational explanations for experimental observations. Given that the O2 •- exhibits a pKb value of 9.12 (characteristic of a typical Brønsted base) [65], it readily undergoes protonation in aqueous environments to form hydroperoxyl radicals (HO2•). Our simulation clearly delineates the dynamic interaction process between CDs and HO2•, which involves: (1) initial formation of hydrogen-bonding networks, (2) subsequent proton and radical transfer leading to adduct formation, and (3) regeneration through renewed hydrogen bonding, proton and radical transfer processes, ultimately completing the catalytic cycle with system restoration to its initial state. Through systematic mechanistic analysis, we have elucidated a dual-path catalytic mechanism for SOD activity of CDs: Path I features CO groups as dominant catalytic centers, while Path II involves synergistic participation of OH and NH2 groups. The entire catalytic cycle is accompanied by the characteristic generation of both O2 and H2O2 (see detailed processes 1 and 2 in Fig. S12).

The Gibbs free energy profiles obtained from DFT calculations (Fig. 3d and e) provide clear visualization of the entire HO2• dismutation process catalyzed by CDs. Comparative analysis reveals that amino functionalization not only optimizes hydrogen-bond network configurations but more importantly significantly enhances proton transfer kinetics, as evidenced by the continuous decrease in Gibbs free energy along the reaction pathway. Theoretical calculations conclusively demonstrate that the synergistic effects of surface (CO, OH, and NH2) functional groups constitute the structural foundation for achieving high-efficiency SOD-like activity, thereby substantially improving ROS scavenging performance. Based on this mechanistic understanding, we rationally designed and synthesized Emo-CDs with precisely tailored surface functional groups, which demonstrated exceptional ROS scavenging performance in systematic evaluations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ROS generation and ROS scavenging of Emo-CDs in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: After demonstrating the efficient ROS generation under WLED illumination and scavenging ability of Emo-CDs, we investigated the antitumor and antioxidation effect of Emo-CDs in vitro. Firstly, we conducted a systematic evaluation of Emo-CDs uptake efficiency using a panel of eight well-characterized cell lines, comprising both malignant and normal phenotypes derived from murine and human tissues. Quantitative flow cytometry analysis demonstrated that Emo-CDs were effectively internalized by all tested cell lines (Fig. S13 and Table S4), with no significant differences in uptake capacity observed between species (mouse/human) or cell types (tumor/normal). This broad-spectrum endocytosis characteristic suggests that Emo-CDs possess potential advantages for application in various disease models. Additionally, we further employed CLSM to visualize the endocytic process of Emo-CDs in 4T1 and HEK293T cells. As shown in Fig. 4 a, after 4 h of incubation, distinct red fluorescence signals were observed in the corresponding channel, confirming successful cellular internalization and intracellular localization of Emo-CDs.

Subsequently, we examined the ability of Emo-CDs to produce ROS within cells. we used SOSG, DHE, and HPF as specific fluorescent probes for 1O2, O2 •-, and •OH, respectively, to detect ROS generation in the cells. As shown in Fig. 4b and S14, after incubating with Emo-CDs, the cells displayed clear green and red fluorescence under WLED irradiation, confirming the generation of 1O2, O2 •-, and •OH following WLED exposure. Compared to other groups, the ROS levels in the Emo-CDs + WLED group were significantly higher than those in the PBS group, the irradiation only (WLED group), and Emo-CDs without WLED exposure (Emo-CDs group). To elucidate the functional role of ROS generation in tumor cell death, we subsequently performed in vitro antitumor efficacy assays to evaluate light-triggered apoptotic effects. First, we assessed the biocompatibility of Emo-CDs and their cell-killing ability under WLED exposure using the MTT assay. The results showed that even at a concentration as high as 200 μg/ml, Emo-CDs did not affect cell growth, as shown in Fig. S15a. We then tested cell survival rates under different light exposure times. As shown in Fig. S15b, cell survival rates decreased as the light exposure time increased, further highlighting the efficient ROS generation ability of Emo-CDs and their excellent antitumor effects. Calcein-AM/PI staining further confirmed the antitumor effect of Emo-CDs. Compared to other groups, the Emo-CDs + WLED group displayed prominent red fluorescence, indicating a large number of apoptotic cells (Fig. S16). To elucidate the antitumor mechanism of Emo-CDs, we performed apoptosis assays using the Annexin V-FITC probe and flow cytometry. As shown in Fig. S17, the green fluorescence intensity in the Emo-CDs + WLED group was significantly higher than in the other control groups, with the apoptosis rate exceeding 90 %. These results indicate that Emo-CDs exhibit excellent antitumor effects under WLED illumination, primarily due to their efficient photodynamic activity.

Under dark conditions, Emo-CDs exhibited outstanding ROS scavenging capabilities. To further explore their role in protecting cells from oxidative stress-induced damage, HEK293T cells were utilized to evaluate the cytotoxicity and cytoprotective effects of Emo-CDs. The CCK-8 assay results showed that Emo-CDs demonstrated no significant cytotoxicity and exhibited excellent biocompatibility at a concentration up to 200 μg/mL (Fig. S18). Further investigations revealed that Emo-CDs provided significant protection against oxidative stress induced by LPS, Fenton's reagent, and H2O2. In the LPS-induced oxidative stress model using HEK293T cells, the cell survival rate increased significantly from 43 % to 91 % with the increasing incubation concentration of Emo-CDs (Fig. 4c). Similarly, in oxidative stress models induced by Fenton's reagent and H2O2, cell survival rates improved from 47 % to 92 % and from 41 % to 88.6 %, respectively (Fig. 4d and e). These findings demonstrate the powerful antioxidative properties of Emo-CDs and their ability to effectively protect cells from oxidative stress-induced damage.

Additionally, we further investigated the role of Emo-CDs in protecting against H2O2-induced cellular oxidative stress using confocal fluorescence microscopy. DCFH-DA and DAF-FM DA probes were employed to detect intracellular ROS and reactive nitrogen species (RNS) levels induced by H2O2. As shown in Fig. 4f and S19, H2O2-induced HEK293T cells exhibited significant green fluorescence signals, indicating the presence of high levels of intracellular ROS and RNS. Satisfactorily, after co-incubation with Emo-CDs, the levels of ROS and RNS returned to that comparable to those of the control group, demonstrating that Emo-CDs effectively scavenge ROS and RNS, thereby exerting antioxidant protective effects. Furthermore, Calcein-AM/PI live/dead cell staining also revealed a significant reduction in cell apoptosis following Emo-CDs treatment (Fig. 4f). To further confirm these findings, Annexin V-FITC probes were used to detect apoptosis, and apoptotic rates were quantified using flow cytometry (Fig. S20). The results additionally evidenced that Emo-CDs effectively protect cells from oxidative stress-induced damage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antitumor effect of Emo-CDs in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the promising antitumor effects observed in vitro, we further investigated the in vivo antitumor efficacy of Emo-CDs. Given the red fluorescence emission properties of Emo-CDs, we assessed their fluorescence imaging capabilities in vivo. As shown in Fig. S21, Emo-CDs generated strong fluorescence signals at the 4T1-induced tumor site, highlighting their potential for fluorescence-guided PDT. To evaluate the antitumor effects of Emo-CDs, we established a mouse subcutaneous tumor model with 4T1 tumor cell inoculation, as illustrated in Fig. 5 a. The mice were randomly divided into four groups (n = 5): PBS, PBS + WLED, Emo-CDs, and Emo-CDs + WLED. Tumor growth was monitored every two days following the intratumoral injection of Emo-CDs and exposure to WLED light. After 14 days, all mice were euthanized, and the tumors were harvested. Ex vivo tumor volume images showed that tumor growth was significantly inhibited in the Emo-CDs + WLED group as evidenced by the smaller tumor size (Fig. 5b). Moderate antitumor efficacy in non-irradiated, Emo-CDs-injected controls might arise from their cationic surface disrupting membrane integrity via electrostatic interactions, combined with ambient light partially activating photosensitive properties to trigger low-level ROS generation. Throughout the treatment period, rapid tumor progression was observed in the PBS control, PBS + WLED, and Emo-CDs alone groups (Fig. 5c). In contrast, the Emo-CDs + WLED combination therapy group demonstrated significant tumor growth suppression, which was further corroborated by the markedly reduced tumor weight measurements (Fig. 5d). Additionally, the body weight of the mice in all four groups gradually increased, with no significant differences observed (Fig. 5e), suggesting that neither Emo-CDs nor WLED had any notable impact on the normal growth of the mice. These results indicate that Emo-CDs, under WLED irradiation, can effectively inhibit tumor growth.

We further evaluated the mechanism of PDT through H&E staining and immunohistochemistry. As shown in Fig. 5f and S22, H&E staining of tumors revealed that the Emo-CDs + WLED group exhibited prominent necrotic areas, while only small areas of necrosis were observed in the other control groups. TUNEL staining (for apoptosis) and Bcl-2 (an anti-apoptotic protein) staining further demonstrated that, in the Emo-CDs + WLED group, the green fluorescence of TUNEL staining was strong and the expression of Bcl-2 was minimal, indicating that PDT promoted tumor apoptosis. Additionally, Ki67 (a nuclear protein associated with cell proliferation) and CD31 (an angiogenesis marker) immunohistochemical staining were used to evaluate the effects of PDT on cell proliferation and tumor angiogenesis. The results showed that, compared to the control groups, the Emo-CDs + WLED group exhibited a significant reduction in Ki67 expression (brown areas in the nuclei), indicating a suppression of tumor proliferation. Moreover, the positive areas of CD31 were significantly reduced in the Emo-CDs + WLED group, suggesting a potential anti-angiogenic effect. Taken together, these staining results indicate that Emo-CDs under light exposure promote apoptosis, inhibit tumor cell proliferation, and suppress angiogenesis, demonstrating excellent antitumor effects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo therapeutic efficacy of Emo-CDs on AKI mice
ce:section.ce:para: AKI is a condition closely associated with ROS [66–68]. Given the excellent ROS scavenging capabilities of Emo-CDs, we further explored their potential in alleviating glycerol-induced AKI in vivo (Fig. 6 a). Due to the size of Emo-CDs, which are nanoparticles smaller than 10 nm, they can be rapidly cleared by the kidneys [69]. This characteristic also suggests that these CDs have the ability to quickly target the kidneys. The accumulation of Emo-CDs in the kidneys is crucial for mitigating kidney damage. Due to the emission peak of Emo-CDs at 675 nm, they can be used as a fluorescent probe to track and identify their distribution in vivo. Therefore, we used an in vivo imaging system to study the distribution of Emo-CDs after tail vein injection in both normal and AKI mice. As shown in Figs. S23 and 6b, the imaging of major organs revealed that after tail vein injection in normal mice, Emo-CDs primarily concentrated in the liver and kidney, with the fluorescence intensity in the kidney reaching its peak 6 h post-injection and still maintaining a certain intensity after 24 h. Furthermore, in AKI mice, Emo-CDs also primarily distributed in the liver and kidney, with the maximum fluorescence intensity observed in the liver and the kidney at 6 h and 12 h post-injection (Fig. 6c), respectively. Notably, after 24 h, Emo-CDs retained strong fluorescence intensity in the kidney, highlighting a significantly higher accumulation of Emo-CDs in the kidney of AKI mice compared to normal mice. In fact, in AKI mice, impaired kidney function reduced the clearance of Emo-CDs, leading to its passive accumulation in the renal tubules and thereby extending the treatment duration. In conclusion, Emo-CDs exhibit excellent specific accumulation and retention capacity in damaged kidneys.

Next, we evaluated the therapeutic potential of Emo-CDs in treating AKI. We measured the serum levels of BUN and CRE across different treatment groups. The results indicated that the BUN and CRE levels in Emo-CD-treated AKI mice were significantly lower than those in untreated AKI mice, with no significant difference compared to normal mice (Fig. 6d and e). The deposition of denatured proteins in renal tubules forms cast structures, which are key indicators of pathological changes. In AKI mice, numerous casts (marked with five-pointed stars) were observed, whereas only a few casts were found in the kidney of Emo-CDs-treated AKI mice, with kidney tissue integrity well preserved (Fig. 6f and Fig. S24a). Compared with the PBS-treated AKI mice group, the ROS levels in the kidneys of AKI mice treated with Emo-CDs were significantly reduced, reaching the levels observed in normal mice (Fig. 6g and S24b). Additionally, as shown in Fig. 6h, AKI mice treated with Emo-CDs survived for more than 14 days, whereas all mice treated with PBS died within 5 days (Fig. 6h). Taken together, these results suggest the significant therapeutic efficacy of Emo-CDs in the treatment of AKI.

We further investigated the molecular mechanism underlying the protective process. Additionally, SOD activity in the Emo-CDs-treated AKI mice group was similar to that in the normal group, while SOD activity in the PBS-treated AKI mice was significantly decreased (Fig. 6i). Furthermore, we measured the levels of two important biomarkers, HO-1 and KIM-1, in the kidney tissues of different treatment groups. As shown in Fig. 6j and k, the levels of KIM-1 and HO-1 in the kidney tissues of Emo-CDs-treated AKI mice were significantly lower than those in the PBS-treated mice. These results suggest that Emo-CDs can effectively protect kidney cells by acting as an antioxidant to scavenge ROS and maintain SOD activity in vivo without light exposure.

Finally, we evaluated the biocompatibility of Emo-CDs in vivo. By analyzing blood chemistry, inflammatory cytokine levels, and histopathological changes in major organs following tail vein injection into healthy mice, we found that no obvious necrosis, congestion, or hemorrhage was observed in the heart, liver, spleen, or lungs at 24 h after a single dose injection (Fig. 7 a). Similarly, no significant inflammation or tissue damage was observed in the glomeruli, tubules, collecting ducts, or ureters of the kidneys (Fig. 7b). Additionally, following the injection of Emo-CDs, serum levels of interleukin-6 (IL-6) and tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α) were comparable to those in the PBS-injected group, indicating that Emo-CDs did not trigger significant immune responses (Fig. 7c). Serum biochemical analysis revealed that aspartate aminotransferase (AST), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), and kidney function markers BUN and CRE were similar to those in the PBS group, demonstrating good biocompatibility with both the liver and kidneys (Fig. 7d and e). Furthermore, complete blood count analysis showed no significant differences between the Emo-CDs and PBS groups (Fig. 7f–j). These results further suggest the good biocompatibility of Emo-CDs in vivo.

In this study, we have successfully prepared a red-emission CDs (Emo-CDs) with a graphene-like structure, whose surface is rich in –OH, NH2 and CO functional groups. Under white LED irradiation, the Emo-CDs efficiently generate ROS and, in the absence of light, can effectively scavenge ROS. Using a series of ROS-specific probes, including SOSG, DHR123, HPF, and ESR spectroscopy, we demonstrated that under white LED irradiation, the Emo-CDs can generate both type I and type II ROS. Tauc plot and valence band XPS analysis revealed that the conduction band redox potential of the Emo-CDs is higher than that of O2/O2 •-, enabling the generation of type I photodynamic process. Furthermore, DFT simulations of two molecular models derived from emodin, with or without N doping, showed that the presence of N atoms in the CDs structure effectively lowers the energy gap between the HOMO and LUMO orbitals, resulting in red emission. The energy gap between S1 and T1 further indicates that N doping enhances the photodynamic process. Finally, in tumor treatment, Emo-CDs combined with WLED effectively inhibited tumor growth. In the absence of light, the Emo-CDs also function as ROS scavengers. WST-1 assays demonstrated that the Emo-CDs possess SOD-like activity. Additionally, the Emo-CDs effectively scavenged •OH and ABTS radicals, as confirmed by ESR spectroscopy. In vitro, when Emo-CDs were added to cells, they effectively protected cells against LPS, Fenton reagent, and H2O2-induced cell damage, significantly reducing intracellular ROS and RNS, and exhibiting good biocompatibility and safety. In in vivo studies, Emo-CDs demonstrated high biological safety, and their red emission capability enabled the identification and tracking of distribution in major organs, offering an effective targeted therapeutic approach for ROS-related diseases. Finally, in the RM-AKI model, Emo-CDs effectively scavenged ROS, protected renal cells, and maintained kidney integrity and function.

CDs exhibiting dual functionality of photoinduced ROS generation and dark-phase ROS scavenging present unique clinical advantages. First, their spatiotemporally controllable ROS regulation enables precise application in photodynamic tumor therapy, achieving effective tumor ablation in irradiated areas while protecting surrounding normal tissues through dark-state ROS scavenging, thereby significantly enhancing treatment safety. Second, these dual-functional CDs are particularly suitable for complex pathological conditions requiring coordinated oxidative stress regulation, such as: (i) combined antibacterial/anti-inflammatory action (via light-induced ROS) and tissue regeneration (via dark-state antioxidation) in diabetic chronic wound treatment, and (ii) dynamic balance regulation of the retinal oxidative microenvironment in ocular diseases. Notably, these sub-10 nm CDs exhibit optimal pharmacokinetic properties: (i) their nanoscale dimensions facilitate efficient renal clearance, minimizing systemic accumulation risks; while (ii) persistent dark-phase antioxidant activity prevents metabolic oxidative stress associated with conventional nanomaterials. These combined attributes confer exceptional biosafety profiles for clinical translation, establishing a robust foundation for therapeutic standardization.
